label: short

Since it's in the dark, I can't see the colors, so I have to consider the worst-case scenario. 

First, let me recall what a matching pair is. A matching pair is two socks of the same color. So, two red, two blue, or two green. Now, the question is asking for two matching pairs. That could be two pairs of the same color (like two red and two blue) or two pairs of different colors (like two red and two blue, or two blue and two green, etc.). Or two red and two red would be two pairs of the same color. But the problem just wants at least two pairs, regardless of color. So, the key is making sure that no matter how the socks are drawn, you end up with two pairs.

To approach this, I think I need to use the pigeonhole principle. The pigeonhole principle is about distributing items into containers and figuring out the minimum number needed to ensure a certain distribution. In the case of socks, the "containers" would be the colors, and the "items" are the socks we pull out.

But first, let's think about what the worst-case scenario is. The worst case is when we pull out as many socks as possible without getting two pairs, and then the next sock will make it so that we have two pairs.
